speaker: interviewer
text: --Paulette Fink, on tape four. February 2, 1996, in Rancho Mirage, California. We were just talking about the movement of getting people to Palestine. And-- and-- and what would-- what happened to these boats? What like-- what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got very, very much involved for somebody who never was a Zionist. And this was very interesting, because in my very first children home, I was getting my budget from the Joint Distribution Committee. I mean, all of us-- we are supported completely by American Jews. And we didn't know anything about how they got the money, and who gave it, or what. But we knew that if America wasn't giving us money, we wouldn't function. But I was only getting 60% of my budget, and I needed more. I need 40-- 40% more. I mean, I couldn't have 100 children and only 60 were taken care of. So I was-- I was really-- I was going to the Joint, and I was asking them from-- I-- I needed more help. And they would say to me, look. You know, you're not the only one. We have an awful lot of other-- other place where we-- that we have to subsidize. We-- our-- our policy is 60%!

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway-- and one night, I went-- just got my budget, and it was on a Friday. And when I got with my money into my first home, there where I lived, I had got no place to put it until Monday morning. And so I-- I-- I did hide it, I mean, somewhere. But I had a lot of money for a whole month.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of a sudden, in the middle of the night, a bang on my front door. And I got panicky. And when I saw that-- I saw, in the shadow, three men. So immediately, I called the police. And the police came. And once they put their big flashlight on those three men, I saw here on their sleeve, Palestinian Brigade. And I got so panicky.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to the policeman-- I said, I'm sorry. I didn't know. I didn't remember they were coming, but I expected them. Now I remember. Yeah, but maybe that's all right. But what about this truck outside? Outside your estate there, there is a big truck full of people. Who are they? So I said-- I-- I-- I didn't say anything else but the truth. They come from Germany. And they are people who are sick and are coming to France in a hospital. Well, they accepted, and they left. But the chief of these three kids look at me, and he said, how is it possible we come into a Jewish home with a Jewish woman at the head of it, and we've gone through the American border, the Russian border, the French border, the English border, and we finally get to Paris, and there you give us up to the police?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm telling you, this was the biggest eye opener I ever had. Because they left me-- the-- all the people in that truck-- they left them in my home with the kid. All the kids got up in the middle of the night. They want to give them their beds. They didn't want to take it. They didn't want to accept a piece of bread. They didn't want to accept anything. They were going home. They were going to erect Israel. They-- they were not going to stay in Paris, not even for a few hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- then we never could do anything with them. But the boys left with their truck, because they had a double bottom at their truck. And between the two bottoms, it was full of ammunitions. And so they didn't want to unload the ammunition in front of them, so they had to go and load the ammunition on a boat, come back to get them, and they left. One of my kid went with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: In any event, I think from that point on, I started to think. And I start to realize that there was more to it than we can really know. And when I went to Germany, to-- I had-- I went on a survey of the DP camps. I was sent by the Joint. And I met [NON-ENGLISH]. And with him I made that trip to go from the DP camps to the shores of Italy. But I-- I decided that the only way to act-- there is no other way to act. I mean, everything has to be made possible to bring those people in. I mean, there is no way otherwise-- no hope. There was no hope for any of them. If you had seen the DPs in the camps, he had lost a wife and two kids. She had lost a husband and four kids. And they were-- the two of them-- completely alone in this world. They got together, and we would see-- in other-- other camp, you would see a woman pregnant. And you knew that she just got pregnant because she lost everything she had before. I mean, you were faced with this all the time. And there was only one way out. There was only one way out, and it was Palestine. And-- and finally, it came to a point where I was-- I-- I was so wrapped up with it, that I did nothing else. I did nothing else. I came-- when I came to America, I-- I-- I start to speak for United Jewish Appeal, and I've been speaking for 40 years. I was National Chairman of Women's Division for three years. And I have never stopped. It's 50 years now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after the war and after you saw what was left, you became very, very Zionist and very, very important to you that-- as-- that these people be able to go to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I mean, you came to the realization that there was no hope. There was-- there was no-- there was no country that these people believed they belonged to-- absolutely none. They hated the thought of Germany. They wanted to get out of there by all means. And they were neither Hungarian, nor Romanian, nor a Czech. They all said-- when you were asking them what they were, they would-- they would answer Jewish. They would-- none of them would say that they had another nationality. They only knew that-- that they were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if you had children in your home, and they didn't want to stay in France, and you were going to-- to-- what did you do with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were taken by these people from Bricha. They were taken under the-- not a fallacy, because it was true. But for them, they were taken-- told that they would be sent-- bring-- they would bring them to the-- the shores and put-- load them on a boat. And that boat would go to Palestine. With the help of God, most of them went to Cyprus. But they still-- they were going to Palestine. They didn't want to stay in Paris more than the few hours that they had to leave them there-- had nothing to do there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and-- and what was it like for these people to get on these boats and go to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was what?

speaker: interviewer
text: The boats to Palestine-- how did-- what happened? Just-- how-- how were they? Did you ever see them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- [LAUGHS] --everything we would possibly put our hands on-- we brought absolutely everything possible. The-- the-- the famous ship, you know, was a ferry boat on the Chesapeake Bay. I mean, it was a boat that was meant for maybe a handful of people. I mean, it was-- it was certainly not meant to cross the Mediterranean. And it was not meant to have the-- the-- the bottom of the boat full of people, even women giving birth on that boat. It was unbelievable. And it was absolutely-- you-- you can't ever imagine what happened on that boat. And they didn't make it. I mean, they made it-- they would have made it if the British didn't stop them. But the British stopped them, and they unload them on-- on three British cruiser. And they came to Marseilles, and the French government offered them asylum. And I was down in Marseilles and trying to plead with them to-- to accept the asylum from the French government. And we'll send them from France back there. No. No. They are going to already Israel. They're not going to France. They're not going anywhere. So we end up in Germany. They end up in that camp there called Poppendorf, and that's where we went to pick up some.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the children-- the children that you-- that you brought into the different homes in France-- did you-- did-- did you get many of them to go-- be able to go to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this became absolutely up to them. It became-- it became what they wanted to do. The ones who want to remain in France-- the France-- the French accept them. I mean, they gave them-- they gave them the nationality if they wanted to. The one who want-- I mean, a lot of Americans want to adopt the children. But my organization was profoundly Zionist, and the only thing that they-- they-- they were willing to do was to send those children to Palestine. They-- they didn't want the children to be scattered around the world, and they-- they-- they thought that in order to remain real Jewish, they had to go to Palestine. So there are a lot of them there, and then a lot of them stayed in France.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it-- how did it come to pass that you came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me?

speaker: interviewer
text: You.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my gosh. There we go. I came-- when I was-- when I was-- I told you that I was really very unhappy about the budget I was getting. I wasn't getting enough money. And so Dr. Schwartz, who was the head of the Joint, internationally at the time, and was stationed in Paris, said to me, OK. So go! Go to America and raise the money!

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, how do you raise the money? I have no idea what you do to raise money. He said, you'll learn. But if you want more money, that's the only way you can get it-- by going there and do it. So I thought I had the right to come for my organization and get money from people and tell them about my children. But then I was told that I had no such-- such right. If I come here, and I speak, I have to speak for the whole organization, not just for my children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I-- I learned what it meant to raise money, and I learned what it meant to be in the United Jewish Appeal Campaign, which, believe me, I had no conception of before-- but none. And I raised a tremendous amount of money by-- by telling my story-- just by telling them my story.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year was it that you came to the United States for the first time? What year did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came January, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who came with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest daughter-- for three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went back to France?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came for three months with a visa for three months. And then at that time, Henry Morgenthau was the President of UJA. And he managed to have my visa extended. But the only thing that they couldn't do was to allow me to go back to France and get a new visa. I couldn't have got one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So because of that, they never let me go back. So I never could go back for two years. For two years, I never went back, never got my youngest daughter. She was-- and she didn't even-- she didn't think that I exist anymore. Where was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was-- at the time when I finally got back, in December '47, I went-- I called my parents from the airport. And they said, well, right now she's at a piano lesson. But by the time you come here, she'll be here. So I took a taxi, went to my parents' home, and she wasn't there yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the doorbell rang, I went to open it. And she looked at me, and she passed me right by. And she said, hi, madam. And so I followed her. And I said, why do you call me madam? She said, because I don't know you. So I say, you sure you don't know me? She said, no, I don't know you. I said, don't you think I could be your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, never. My mother went way, way, way back there in America. You know, really far away. She abandoned me. She doesn't want me anymore. I don't have a mother anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you think I took that easily-- this was one of the worst things that ever happened. And it took her three, four days to decide it must be it. And then she said, then I'm going to America now? I said, yes. Then during the night she-- she banged on my-- my-- and she said to me, Mommy, if I go to America now, can you teach me English overnight?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is the way-- So the next time--

speaker: interviewee
text: --this ended.

speaker: interviewer
text: She came with you the next time?

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was a very painful experience-- very painful. I don't know if nobody told her that I was really there, that I existed, that I didn't abandon her. But in her mind-- in her mind I had abandoned her, and that's all she knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where had she been the last two years? Where had she--

speaker: interviewee
text: With my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she living with my in-laws for awhile, but lived mainly with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because when you left, you thought you would be back in three months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course! Never left for any more than three months. I would never have left. I would never have left. And I couldn't take them both. I mean, there was no way I could take them both. And my parents were here at that time. So I thought, well, if they decide not to come back to France, at least they'll see one. I can always come back with the other later. But then they-- they listened to me, and they went back. And so they-- they went back to France in that-- in June, 1946. And then they-- then they took her over. But you see, you have to-- to realize how every life-- every life in France was deteriorated. I mean, there was-- there was nobody who hadn't been affected. There was nobody who didn't lose people. There wasn't anybody who-- if you had seen the Day of Liberation in Paris, there wasn't a dry eye. There wasn't a dry eye on the Champs-Elysee with-- they had a big parade over there, all the American tanks coming down, and the French-- the Free Army coming down.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was-- everybody was-- was-- was crying. Why? Because there wasn't one soul who didn't lose people-- not one. There was nobody intact. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So did you come back to the United States again then after you went back? When did--

speaker: interviewee
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: You went back, and you got your second daughter. And you-- and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. In December, '47, when I went on a survey of the DP camps and when she didn't recognize me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I saw her for a week, and I left again. I went through Germany, and Austria, and Czechoslovakia. I went to all the camps. I went to Theresienstadt. I went to Auschwitz-- everywhere. And I came back to get her in March. Then I brought her over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Brought her to the United States with you? And did you stay in the United States then for-- after that-- always?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, more or less-- more or less. I was always going back. I always had to come for the next campaign. I mean, it-- she just won't let me go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from then on, you always worked for the United Jewish Appeal?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: From then on-- you.

speaker: interviewee
text: All along-- 50 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what different things have you done?

speaker: interviewee
text: What? When?

speaker: interviewer
text: You've been head of the women's division for the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was National Chairman of the Women's Division for three years-- a long time ago-- '60, '61, '62. It's a long time ago. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But there was a great satisfaction, you know, in knowing what we were doing. We were building a country. We were rescuing people. We were-- we-- we had one immigration after another. I mean, we-- we rescued people who might never have been rescued without us. I mean, so it's-- it been our entire work from-- from the beginning of the German occupation has been a world of rescue and nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did you meet your second husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I-- I was in Minneapolis once visiting with friends that I met at the very beginning I came here. And he was there for dinner. They introduced me to him, and that's the way I got married to an American, which I never thought I would do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you marry him?

speaker: interviewee
text: 40 years ago--

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: --been married 40 years. All my grandchildren were born here. All my great-grandchildren were born here. And I have a lot of them. I have nine great-grandchildren and eight grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are they all in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're all here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your second husband-- he died when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three years ago. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do we do? We let it go? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the last 40 years, Paulette Fink, you have done many, many things. But if I were to ask you, for you, what some of the most important highlights have been, what would you mention?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, you finally believe that-- that the word rescue is really part of your life. I mean, that's-- that's finally all there is, because after the war and after we started to do real, constructive work, and after Israel became a state, and we started to send people to Israel, do you know that the first year that the state was created, we brought in 1,000 people every day? Every day for a whole year, 365 days, we brought 1,000 people. And so many of us were involved in-- in actually rescuing those people who finally end up in Israel. And I, myself-- I was involved in the illegal immigration of Morocco and took a lot of people out of Morocco and put them on little boats, and put-- they sent them-- we sent them to Gibraltar. And in Gibraltar already, they were picked up by-- at that one time, Israel had a boat called the and the would slow down at the level of Gibraltar. And we would go with little boats and put those people on board ship. And then the business of settling them in Israel, and not being happy at all, and being very, very critical, and very, and-- and very unwilling to really settle. A lot of them had to go back. A lot of them went back, and they only realized then what they had in Israel, and they come back. But at the beginning, it was a very rough experience. And then in the Operation Magic Carpet brought the Yemenites-- brought the first Yemenites in Israel. That was also fabulous. I became very attached to the Yemenites-- wonderful people, very, very crafted, very good people. And then we transported people from Romania, and from Hungary, and-- and from Czechoslovakia. We-- we did nothing but rescue. I mean, really, the whole-- my whole world is rescuing people. And-- and I have such-- such tremendous satisfaction when I go to Israel every year that I see-- I see what happened to them, how they settled down, and how-- how things prospered for-- with them. And it's really-- it's really-- it was worth all the nightmares and all the-- and all the trouble. But it's a life that you very-- very hard to give up. You see, I reach a point where I-- I just cannot travel as much, and I can no more go speak so much. But I do the best I can. And it's very interesting, because now we are building everything on continuity. And continuity for us is real, because my youngest daughter is on the National Board of UJA. My granddaughter is on the Young Leadership, so the three of us have gone together and speak. And we speak on continuity. And this is the main thing. Continuity is really where-- where it's leading us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about talking today? What about doing this video and your experience during the war? If you were gonna make a few closing remarks about your experience in the resistance and-- and what happened to you during the war, what would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: What counted more? Listen. I mean, today-- today I mean, I have a lot to be grateful for. I have-- I have eight grandchildren. I have nine great-grandchildren. I mean, what-- what else can you hope for? And I've reached a time in my life where I thank God I'm healthy enough to continue and to still-- to still go on, because it's something that you get so involved in that you can't stop it like this. It's not-- it's almost impossible. I couldn't live a life without being involved, more or less, but be involved. And so God has been very good to me, because he gave me enough health to go on and surrounded by all the children. I mean, it-- it's really a fabulous satisfaction when you think of what could have been, and you think of what you finally lived through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very happy to have been here. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Back on with Paulette Fink, who has with her her daughter, Nadine. So let's introduce her. Paulette, this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think that they'd like to know a little bit about-- what do you remember-- how much do you remember from the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember quite a bit from the war-- perhaps not as much as if I had been older, but certainly I remember certain incidents, that I, as a matter of fact, I've-- going to talk about in mid March for a group of pioneer women in Boston, where I live. And I have written quite a lot about what I think I remember. Sometimes maybe they're a little bit fantasized, but on the whole, I think I do remember quite, quite a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you remember your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, absolutely, absolutely.

speaker: interviewee
text: She remembers her father very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: I could tell you what I remember, but I don't know how much you want me to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just-- just in-- in two or three minutes, describe the most vivid memories that you have of him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of him, in particular.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of him, in particular.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's do that first.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. Well, the memories of my father are dear ones, of course, because I didn't know him very well. But I do remember him as being a very kind, and thoughtful, and sensitive man who was very concerned about his daughters and very anxious to see as much of us as he could, but could not, because of obvious reasons, which my mother described.

speaker: interviewer
text: And yet at times being stern-- for instance, if I did not do my homework on math and what we call arithmetic in those days-- and still do-- he would slap me a little bit and say, what I-- why is it that you're not doing? And you must do. Because he was an extremely conscientious man and wanted us to do well, even though he wasn't there to really help us in-- in our upbringing-- felt very strongly about doing for France what he should be doing for France.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- I understood, but not terribly well-- what was happening, but very happy to see him when we-- he did come back. I can't say that the memories are terribly vivid. They're not. When he did die, and we went to the gravesite, I-- I look at the picture and realize what it meant to both my sister and myself. But of course, my being older, it was very, very difficult-- very difficult to realize that we would never see him again.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what had happened to him specifically, my mother did not tell us for being so young. What would we understand as to being tortured like he was tortured? And I'm sure she did not want that to be known to us until we became older. What about the war itself? In a few words, what-- what is the atmosphere? What are some of the memories that stand out as to just the feeling of what it was like during the war years for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. OK. I can tell you that we lived in a house, which I thought was really quite pleasant for-- with a big garden. And that was very vivid in my mind, because I remember playing with my friends, and my cousins, and picking fruit and vegetables, and going to share those with the farmers who would in turn give us. That was a very vivid memory and a happy memory, because those people-- the peasants in that area of France were very, very kind. Another vivid memory was my grandfather Opper, who was my father's father and a doctor. And I remember specifically his treating the peasants when they had hand problems with puss on their hands. I remember they're being in-- in a corridor, waiting to be cared for by my grandfather. And I thought how wonderful that he could do that for them, because there was no real hospital or any other good doctor around. And I say today that it's because of him that we're alive, because nobody denounced us in the town, even though everybody knew we were Jewish. And I'll tell you why. I-- I know as a child that everybody knew we were Jewish. We went to church. And we did-- my sister and I-- we did what we should do as good quote unquote Catholic girls. But the one thing we did not do was kiss the bishop's ring.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And the kids noticed that and said to-- and one child, on the way back to home-- and I'm going to describe this to pioneer women in Boston-- said to me, you're different from other people-- other youngsters here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, what do you mean? And she said, my ancestors walked on this earth, on this ground that we walk on. But yours didn't. And, of course, I did not understand at all what she meant.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is the-- these are the kinds of very vivid memories that I-- I didn't know what-- what religion I was. It meant nothing to me. I do remember that one of our--

speaker: interviewee
text: But you remember what she said to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, I just said it.

speaker: interviewee
text: You come from a--

speaker: interviewee
text: A different background.

speaker: interviewee
text: --from the wandering Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, she said it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Come from the wandering Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these-- these impressions that there was something underlying are with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, absolutely-- absolutely. Or seeing a man-- I think either this child or another child. You know something happened on a train last week. My mother told me about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what happened on that train? A man with a hook nose was taken by the Germans. You understand why he was taken? Because he had a hook nose. It meant he was Jewish. That was another very vivid memory. And so these things just happened, and they-- and then they just happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they happened, and then they just happened. We tried to lead as normal a life as a little girl would like to lead-- going to school haphazardly, not on a regular basis. Because every once in a while we would have to go quickly, take the bikes and go. And we-- and she-- my mother would take us and say, now we're going. And then we would scream and yell and say, oh, how wonderful. We're going! We're going somewhere nice! And she said, shh, shh, shh. Don't say anything. We can't-- nobody has to-- must know where we're going. We have to hide. So these, also, are our memories. I look back at my pictures when I was a child, and I don't think they're very happy pictures. I mean, I was overly sensitive, as I still am, even though I'm 60 years old today. I take everything to heart, and the pictures of myself when I was in those days are pretty sad-- sad face.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're waiting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's-- why don't you help us by-- by-- let's do of the pictures, Bob.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: A picture of my husband and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Start over.

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: [FRENCH]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my husband and myself, in 1942, in Le Chambon, where my children were very often and where-- where we-- we were that day. And at that time, he was-- he was already taken a few days from working in the resistance. And we went out from Saint-Etienne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just name them left to right-- first name and last name. No?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my husband, Yves Opper, myself, Paulette Opper, my two daughters-- Nadine, my oldest, and Francelyne, my youngest, sitting on the wall on the bridge over a little river called Le Lignon. And the town name was Le Chambon-sur-Lignon.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go. Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Coming from the left, my youngest daughter, Francelyne with her father, Yves Opper, whom-- it was almost the last visit with us very shortly before he was killed; myself; my mother-in-law, which is Opper; my brother-in-law, George Opper; his wife, Valia; my oldest daughter, Nadine; Jacqueline-- Jacqueline, my-- my husband's youngest sister; the maid who stayed with us the whole war and never wanted to leave us; and Nicole, the-- oh, the-- the-- the-- the-- the my husband's sister; Dr. Opper, my father-in-law, holding in his arms little Remi, who was my brother-in-law's child.
